narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, looking at factors like grades, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, LSAT score of 25.50, Year 1 school decile of 3rd out of 10, Year 3 decile of 2nd out of 10, middle family income bracket, and plan to attend full-time—and predicted a 43.2% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try. This elevated risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. Among all the factors considered, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive influence, actually pushing the prediction toward you passing rather than failing, which is a bright spot in your profile. Next in importance was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which had a notable negative pull, nudging the model toward expecting a bar failure because it was quite low at the 2nd decile compared to the typical student's 5.5th decile position. Your LSAT score ranked third in influence and also contributed toward the failure prediction, as your 25.50 was substantially below the average of 36.70 seen in the dataset. While your GPA held things back from a worse outcome and your full-time status aligns with most successful students, those lower deciles and LSAT score tipped the scales against you relative to peers who typically rank higher academically and score stronger on entrance exams. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially given your solid GPA and middle-income background that many students share. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, as that could significantly shift future predictions in your favor. Strengthening your recent academic decile through additional coursework or a post-bac program might also help demonstrate upward momentum. Reach out to admissions advisors for feedback on targeted improvements, and explore schools with holistic reviews that weigh your unique strengths as an Asian male applicant more flexibly. With focused effort on these areas, you can reshape your profile for stronger outcomes next time.
